# Research Notes: Rhetorical Devices

## Step
- Step 2 of Plan (20260401-concurrent-01)

## Sources Used
- src-003 (BYU Rhetoric — Figures of Speech) — ACQ-002, passed
- src-004 (Stanford Encyclopedia — Aristotle's Rhetoric) — ACQ-002, passed

## Key Findings

### Metaphor
An implicit comparison asserting one thing IS another (no "like"/"as"). Aristotle called it "the mark of genius" — the ability to perceive resemblances between dissimilar things. Operates by transferring qualities from the "vehicle" to the "tenor." Extended metaphors sustain comparison across multiple sentences or an entire work. Aristotle's taxonomy: genus-to-species, species-to-genus, species-to-species, analogy (proportional metaphor — the most effective) (src-003, src-004).

### Anaphora
Repetition of a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, sentences, or lines. Creates rhythm and emphasis, building rhetorical momentum. One of the most powerful devices in persuasive oratory — the repeated phrase becomes a rhythmic anchor reinforcing the central message. Example: "We shall fight on the beaches, we shall fight on the landing grounds..." (Churchill) (src-003).

### Parallelism
Use of similar grammatical structures in related words, phrases, or clauses. Creates balance, rhythm, and clarity. Operates at multiple levels: word, phrase, clause, sentence. Fundamental to effective prose — aids comprehension through predictable patterns and creates aesthetic order. Example: "I came, I saw, I conquered" (tricolon) (src-003).

### Antithesis
Places two contrasting ideas in parallel structure within a single sentence. Juxtaposition of opposites creates emphasis and clarity. Aristotle: "contraries are easily understood and even more so when placed side by side" (Rhetoric III.9). Works by making contrasts explicit through structural symmetry. Examples: "best of times, worst of times" (Dickens), "one small step for a man, one giant leap for mankind" (Armstrong) (src-003, src-004).

### Additional Devices
- **Simile**: explicit comparison using "like" or "as" (src-003)
- **Metonymy**: substituting a name with something closely associated (src-003)
- **Epistrophe**: repetition at the end of successive clauses — mirror of anaphora (src-003)
- **Chiasmus**: reversed parallel structure (A-B-B-A pattern). Example: "Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country" (JFK) (src-003)

### Aristotle on Style
Prose should have rhythm but not meter. The paean is the most suitable prose rhythm because it is the least recognizable as verse (src-004).
